item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
" I have learned that this device delivers a mild euphoric sensation when latched onto the human body."

Am I the only one that thought the implications of that line were somewhat…"inappropriate", shall we say?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely a great expansion on this nice little SCP

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bluewhisper just produced an ASMR video in which she demonstrates the process by which SCP-1519 is created.

I've seen SCP ASMR before, but this is the first one I've seen that doesn't either gloss over the Foundation lore or simply consist of an article reading in a soft voice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Construction of an instance of SCP-1519 takes an average of 10 minutes with proper preparations.

I feel like the weight and implications of this are generally overlooked when this article is read. This process turns a human being into a battery in ten minutes?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: All other criticisms aside the only thing keeping me from an upvote is the lack of clarity in precisely how it's able to power anything regardless of the receiving end's bits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a creepy little thing, but the description is confusing, and I'm not sure I can figure out what it does.

To wit, the paragraph starting with "SCP-1519 generates an electrical charge" goes immediately into talking about instances the Foundation has created. But it's not mentioned until two paragraphs later that this is something that can be done. Also, it's stated the heart is the only organ, but then the second, artificial organ is mentioned.

Needs some work to avoid these logical confoundments.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, an artificial organism that turned out to be usable as a battery of some sort..
nice - even nicer when I imagine this thing biting electrode and making light bulbs flash.
(that's the image the author intended, I hope)

If it is reproducible, Foundation scientists could have put an extra work on it and found out how it works, then this thing could go to EX category - of course, I recognize the major problem of not having this in real life, so maybe you could have made it basically more 'mysteric'. The text make it seem all too possible, if you see what I mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How the hell did they find out it's Da Vinci simply via testing and who had the idea to turn him in a battery, considering that, I assume, the subject must be alive for the procedure?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the implication was they dissected one after it ran out of power. It mentions that they become completely inactive (i.e., dead) after they run out of power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1519 may not be constructed with human parts.

SCP-1519 is a small cylindrical biological system weighing on average .5 kg, constructed of human joints, tendons […]

"may" and "can" are not the same, and I'd actually recommend putting that back into the containment procedure.

"Can" means "capable of doing". "May" means "allowed to do". In this case, the containment procedure (formerly) called for not making any more human instances.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's just the thing. The human body doesn't produce THAT much electricity. Not enough that you could plug a lamp into yourself and run it for over a month.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is a GREAT creepy, weird idea that could use a little bit of work in its execution.

I love the idea that just by cutting up a person with crude tools, placing this organ here, some teeth there, a piece of the brain here, you can suddenly have a unique "creature" that provides a source of paranormal electricity and/or euphoria. Like if this were real, how many people would have discovered it by now? Not many! I doubt in all of human history there have been too many people simply cutting up fresh bodies and connecting the parts in random a order. There's the hint that its converting a dead person's soul into electricity. There's the idea that if made one 100% perfectly, like the Ancient Chinese did, you could have one last forever, but alas, we can't recreate their perfection.

I would personally get rid of the idea that it works on any animal. I would have it only work with human parts. There would add a tiny bit to the supernatural theme (you need a soul or something otherwise 'special' to power it) and it would also get rid of the reader wondering why no one else has found this. If it works with any animal parts, chances are there's some weirdo out there in the last 100 years working in a slaughterhouse who has tried all the different combinations.

The article also needs a little bit of cleanup. "SCP-1519 generates an electrical charge." should be "Instances of SCP-1519" so we know it's not just the one. Then you jump from paragraph to another where in one paragraph you are talking about Instances of SCP-1519, to talking about the specific, original SCP-1519 and it gets confusing. You might call the original SCP-1519-1 to help avoid confusion. You also need to change your ordering around. You should discuss the original, then the copies, or the copies then the original, but not all together in a mess like you have it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh god oh god

Testing reveals the parts belonging to one ████████ ██ █████.

eight letters, two letters, five letters…

Leonardo Da Vinci.

My god, what have you done

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We have a lot of "stuff made of human body parts" so that doesn't earn any automatic crativity points— more importantly, though, unless I'm just dense it is still unclear to me what this thing actually DOES. There's technical language and then there's obfustication. Also:

It is possible to duplicate SCP-1519 artificially with access to the appropriate human, or nonhuman tissue and a capable specialist.

Would anyone ever attempt this WITHOUT the aid of a "specialist"? Specialist is a silly word in this context, anyway.

The ‘lips’ on the ends of SCP-1519 reflexively form a conductive, vacuum sealed barrier to facilitate the flow of generated from SCP-1519.

The flow of what? What is generated?

No vote, for now. This is just kinda meh and convoluted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I tried to imply that he was attempting to improve the thing and when he died the schematics for the new version worked horribly well. I will revise those other parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That was disturbing.

Upvoted.

Also, I like the little hints you put in. The blanked out owner is Leonardo Da Vinci, isnt it? What gave it away was the SCP number. 1519 is Leonardo's year of death.

Could use a little cleaning up though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That gives me an idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the concept, but the execution is currently rather confusing; do I understand that the 'still active' instance of SCP-1519 is one of the 'far superior' originals and 500 years old? The letter seems to imply even these originals wear down: only one of the originals was still active in 1500, and then only because it used younger/healthier organs than the Italian inventor was able to use 'with a sound conscience'. Also, you mention the original has to be fed three times daily, which presumably didn't happen while it was buried. I also don't seem to be able to get my head around this:

The ‘lips’ on the ends of SCP-1519 reflexively form a conductive, vacuum sealed barrier to facilitate the flow of generated from SCP-1519.

I presume this should say 'generated charge' or similar, but why does it need a vacuum? Additionally, you say the device will deactivate after being drained of unreplenishable power, but the 'original' is still working - should the age reference and the blanked-out name mean something that would explain this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Frankenbattery, basically? I like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article's a mess and the concept is iffy. I could see liking this if it where written a bit better, but I think the foundation has a few organic/made of human body parts items already.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Dissection of the object reveals no anomalous occurrence or materials."

- this should be vivisection, to make it clear that the object is still "alive".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a lot to like about this — that we've been replicating them, the letter at the end — but I cannot in good conscience upvote until this thing has gotten some proofreading. There are words missing, even.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Maybe have the Italian dude explain that he's using them for light bulbs with more clarity. I think you mean that the writer is using the conductors to power filaments in glass bulbs, but someone could mistake their use as a lighter (think fire).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The "…parts belong to…" line just seems randomly out of place and irrelevant to anything around it.

I'm confused; do they make electricity, or some sort of fuel, or what? Because when they are used as "lamplighters" in the renaissance, that makes me think of lamp oil or some-such. But the rest of the description made me think electricity.

Still, my gut reaction was positive.

Post Edited: Because the grammer mistakes were fixed before I could list them and I fail at spell-check. The rest stands.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you this is helpful. I'll include some testing tables in the rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did some rearranging of some elements and hopefully I clarified some things.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, what's that C stand for again?

There are enough unknowns in the equation to bring cause for concern here, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not sure if I like this or not, but I wanted to share a few comments:

SCP-1519 are constructed and disposed of per test with Site Director approval.

This sentence seems clumsy to me. Are researchers allowed to make more of them? I'm not sure what "per test" means here?

Access to SCP-1519 blueprints are limited to Researchers …

Access is limited to researchers (no capitalization)

SCP-1519 may not be constructed with human parts.
SCP-1519 is a small cylindrical biological system weighing on average .5 kg, constructed of human joints, tendons […]

These two sentences contradict each other.

It is possible to duplicate SCP-1519 artificially with access to the appropriate human,

Who is the appropriate human? Einstein? Stephen Hawking? My mom?

SCP-1519s

In both cases, "SCP-1519s" should be "SCP-1519's" because it indicates possession (of the digestive materials and the mouths).

man - is is

Double "is" (nitpicky, yep, sorry).

Perhaps with some additional cleanup, I could learn to like this more than I do. No vote at present.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I get what it's getting at, I feel that it just isn't executed well enough as the concept deserves. Withholding vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: FOUNDATION BATTERIES ARE MADE OF PEOPLE

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with previous critiques that tone and wording could be cleaned up. In particular, it sounds like you have two items being retained by the Foundation - the original SCP-1519, and the blueprints. But your containment procedures don't list anything regarding the apparatus itself, and the blueprints are not given a designation (e.g. SCP-1519-1).

It also bothers me that this is an easily reproducible item (takes 10 minutes1) that somehow creates an anomalous organ upon completion. Whether biological or mechanical, it doesn't make sense that the Foundation would assemble something according to a schematic and all of a sudden not understand what they'd created. It would make more sense to me if the other experiments were described by notes found with the apparatus rather than as Foundation experiments, and if the article noted that all attempts by the Foundation to create a functioning SCP-1519 via the blueprints have failed (say, with the final structure failing to function/missing a crucial element/something-or-other).

It's not bad. It could be better.

Footnotes
1. Which, btw, is in and of itself unbelievable - at minimum you're talking about (effectively) reconstructive surgery, not changing a battery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But I'm not convinced it's anomalous. I upvoted because it is neat and creepy, but I think the anomalous effect has to be stated a bit more clearly. Otherwise it's just a clever invention that uses organic parts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: From what I can tell, it's an organic battery that eats food and produces electricity. Neat, useful, and horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This needs some serious cleanup, but I like what you're driving at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Could be an interesting mental image if I could better figure out what's going on. Right now it's fairly unclear.

Also, shouldn't God be capitalized in the translated letter if the epistle dates from a stonework time period?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, not sure how I feel about this one. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The note transcripted from Italian, unnecessary portions edited and revised for readability." <— missing a word in there, probably "The note was transcripted…" Otherwise, I'm not yet sure how I feel on this article. I'll mull it over for a while and come back.

EDIT: Also, does it produce or leech electricity? I was a bit confused as far as that when I read the article…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I upvoted for the concept, but I'm not 100% sure why it was acquired by the Foundation at all.
Does the design somehow break the laws of physics? Is some kind of demonic invocation required?
If it's just a novel way to arrange organic material for a beneficial purpose, there's no reason to lock it up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An organic generator.
How does that work? Scientifically it makes no sense whatsoever, but its creepy and it seems like you put some work into writing this.
Upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not nearly as corny as the SECRET BODY PART that you have right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Regardless of that, but thanks, noted.

I'm referring to the organ constructed of other organs, I just don't want to give it a name as that is sort of corny to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really. You're saying that apparently there is some part of the human body that is SO SECRET that it gets redacted from the normal report.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll stand by the other critiques, but this is silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hippocampus, cartilage, teeth, [REDACTED],

Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the concept, but the wording could use some untangling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well I think that it's a nice concept but it didn't grab me very well spice it up a bite. Also there are no tags I'm not going to down vote just for that, but it's going to make other people down vote. No vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Made a few changes, a bit more later.

It's supposed to be

...
...

Leonardo Da Vinci

and he is the SCP-XXX, which is still functional. I didn't mention there being any other instances, only blueprints and an original.

Yes. All it does is produce electricity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-1519s mouths, or openings, can be fed into virtually any power source. The ‘lips’ on the ends of SCP-1519 reflexively form a conductive, sealed barrier to facilitate the flow of electricity. SCP-1519 will detach from a feed only when SCP-1519 has lost a sufficient amount of power. SCP-1519 cannot be reactivated after it has been fully drained of power.

I don't understand what you are saying here. Feed *into* a power source?
Also tonefail much like Tom mentioned.
Overall, this isn't a bad concept but needs to be written better than this, and that's me who loves biofuckery of this kind , usually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little confused as to what this SCP is. It's a collection of organs that… produce electricity when assembled correctly?

chitinous bits

Poor word choice here for a technical document.

Construction of an instance of SCP-1519 takes an average of 10 minutes with proper preparations.

This seems a little too glib. You don't really mention what one has to do to construct SCP-1519, or what must be done to prepare (other than harvesting organs, I assume.)

SCP-1519s mouths, or openings, can be fed into virtually any power source. The ‘lips’ on the ends of SCP-1519 reflexively form a conductive, sealed barrier to facilitate the flow of electricity. SCP-1519 will detach from a feed only when SCP-1519 has lost a sufficient amount of power. SCP-1519 cannot be reactivated after it has been fully drained of power.

How does it plug into a standard AC socket? Does it have metal tongues that hook themselves into the system? (And, correct me if I'm wrong, but I don't think you need a vacuum to allow for electrical flow.)

Note found in stonework jar in Florence, Italy at the place of SCP-1519’s discovery

This is the part that I thought was the most interesting. I want to know more about this guy- what drove him to create this biomachine? How did he die? I like to think of him as being some kind of alternate-universe Luigi Galvani.

or if the device is drained, a Scottish delicacy.

This line made me chuckle.

Overall, the description and some of the technical parts are vague and confusing to the point where I think I might be misreading the whole thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok let's see

Testing reveals the parts belonging to one ████████ ██ █████.

I feel like this should be later in the article. It seems like you are describing the original subject.
